content.text: Common Az PowerShell Cmdlets

Az cmdlets follow a consistent Verb-AzNoun naming pattern. Once you know the noun for a resource type (AzVM, AzStorageAccount, AzWebApp) and the standard verbs (Get, New, Set, Remove, Start, Stop), you can predict most cmdlet names without looking them up. This section walks through the most-used cmdlets organized by resource area.

attrs.alt: Az cmdlet verb-noun naming convention with examples

content.text: Discovering Cmdlets
PowerShell's Get-Command and Get-Help are the primary tools for exploring the Az module. You do not need to memorize every cmdlet—you need to know how to find and read them.

# List all Az cmdlets containing "VM"
Get-Command -Module Az.Compute -Name "*VM*" | Select-Object Name

# List all Get- cmdlets across the Az module
Get-Command -Verb Get -Module Az.* | Select-Object Name, Module | Sort-Object Name

# Find cmdlets for a resource type by keyword
Get-Command -Module Az.* | Where-Object Name -like "*KeyVault*"

# Read full help for a cmdlet (includes all parameters and examples)
Get-Help New-AzVM -Full

# Show only the examples
Get-Help Get-AzResourceGroup -Examples

# Show parameter details
Get-Help Set-AzContext -Parameter Subscription
Subscription and Context Cmdlets

# Show current subscription and account
Get-AzContext

# List all subscriptions your identity has access to
Get-AzSubscription | Select-Object Name, Id, State

# Switch subscription
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"
# Or by name:
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionName "Production"

# Check the active subscription after switching
(Get-AzContext).Subscription.Name

# List all saved contexts (if you have multiple accounts logged in)
Get-AzContext -ListAvailable | Select-Object Name, Account
Resource Group Cmdlets

# List all resource groups
Get-AzResourceGroup | Select-Object ResourceGroupName, Location, ProvisioningState

# Filter by location
Get-AzResourceGroup | Where-Object Location -eq "eastus"

# Get a specific resource group
Get-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG

# Create a resource group
New-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG -Location eastus -Tag @{Environment="Dev"; Team="Platform"}

# Update tags on a resource group (replaces all tags)
Set-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG -Tag @{Environment="Prod"; CostCenter="1234"}

# Delete a resource group and all its contents
Remove-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG -Force

# Delete without waiting for completion
Remove-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG -Force -AsJob
Resource Cmdlets (Generic)
Az.Resources cmdlets work on any resource type using the -ResourceType parameter, making them useful for generic automation across services.

# List all resources in a resource group
Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object Name, ResourceType, Location

# Filter by resource type
Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName myRG -ResourceType "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines"

# Get a specific resource by ID
Get-AzResource -ResourceId "/subscriptions/$subId/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/..."

# Search resources across the subscription by tag
Get-AzResource -Tag @{Environment="Prod"} | Select-Object Name, ResourceType

# Move resources between resource groups
$resourceIds = (Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName sourceRG).ResourceId
Move-AzResource -DestinationResourceGroupName destRG -ResourceId $resourceIds

# Apply tags to a resource
$resource = Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myStorage
$resource.Tags["CostCenter"] = "9999"
Set-AzResource -ResourceId $resource.ResourceId -Tag $resource.Tags -Force
Virtual Machine Cmdlets

# List all VMs in a resource group
Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG | Select-Object Name, Location, VmId

# Get a single VM object
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM

# Read VM properties
$vm.HardwareProfile.VmSize       # SKU e.g. Standard_D2s_v5
$vm.StorageProfile.OsDisk.Name   # OS disk name
$vm.NetworkProfile.NetworkInterfaces[0].Id  # NIC resource ID

# Get VM power state (requires -Status flag)
Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM -Status |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty Statuses |
    Where-Object Code -like "PowerState/*" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty DisplayStatus

# Start / stop / restart
Start-AzVM   -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM
Stop-AzVM    -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM -Force
Restart-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM

# Resize a VM
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM
$vm.HardwareProfile.VmSize = "Standard_D4s_v5"
Update-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -VM $vm

# Run a script on a VM (no SSH/RDP needed)
Invoke-AzVMRunCommand -ResourceGroupName myRG -VMName myVM `
    -CommandId RunShellScript `
    -ScriptString "hostname && df -h"

# Deallocate a VM (stops billing for compute)
Stop-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM -Force
# Note: -Force on Stop-AzVM also deallocates (not just OS shutdown)
Storage Account Cmdlets

# List storage accounts in a resource group
Get-AzStorageAccount -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object StorageAccountName, Location, SkuName, Kind

# Create a storage account
New-AzStorageAccount `
    -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -Name mystorage123 `
    -Location eastus `
    -SkuName Standard_LRS `
    -Kind StorageV2 `
    -AccessTier Hot

# Get the storage account key
$keys = Get-AzStorageAccountKey -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name mystorage123
$key1 = $keys[0].Value

# Create a storage context for blob/queue/table operations
$ctx = New-AzStorageContext -StorageAccountName mystorage123 -StorageAccountKey $key1

# List containers
Get-AzStorageContainer -Context $ctx

# Upload a blob
Set-AzStorageBlobContent -Container "mycontainer" -File ".\data.csv" `
    -Blob "uploads/data.csv" -Context $ctx

# Download a blob
Get-AzStorageBlobContent -Container "mycontainer" -Blob "uploads/data.csv" `
    -Destination ".\data.csv" -Context $ctx
Key Vault Cmdlets

# Create a Key Vault
New-AzKeyVault -Name myKeyVault -ResourceGroupName myRG -Location eastus

# Get a secret value (plain text)
$secret = Get-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName myKeyVault -Name mySecret -AsPlainText

# Set a secret
$secureValue = ConvertTo-SecureString "mysecretvalue" -AsPlainText -Force
Set-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName myKeyVault -Name mySecret -SecretValue $secureValue

# List all secrets (names and metadata only — not values)
Get-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName myKeyVault | Select-Object Name, ContentType, Enabled

# Grant a service principal access to secrets
Set-AzKeyVaultAccessPolicy `
    -VaultName myKeyVault `
    -ObjectId $sp.Id `
    -PermissionsToSecrets get,list,set,delete

# Delete a secret (soft delete — recoverable for 90 days by default)
Remove-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName myKeyVault -Name mySecret
# Purge (permanent delete, only if soft-delete protection allows)
Remove-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName myKeyVault -Name mySecret -InRemovedState -Force
Working with Cmdlet Output in the Pipeline

Az cmdlets return rich .NET objects, not text. This means PowerShell's pipeline verbs (Where-Object, Select-Object, Sort-Object, ForEach-Object, Group-Object, Measure-Object) work directly on the returned objects without parsing.

# Filter VMs by size using Where-Object
Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Where-Object { $_.HardwareProfile.VmSize -like "Standard_D*" }

# Select specific properties and rename them
Get-AzStorageAccount |
    Select-Object @{N="Name";E={$_.StorageAccountName}},
                  @{N="SKU";E={$_.Sku.Name}},
                  Location

# Sort resource groups by name
Get-AzResourceGroup | Sort-Object ResourceGroupName

# Count resources per type
Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Group-Object ResourceType |
    Select-Object Count, Name |
    Sort-Object Count -Descending

# Sum total number of VMs across all resource groups
(Get-AzVM).Count

# Pipe directly into Remove (with confirmation prompt)
Get-AzResourceGroup | Where-Object ResourceGroupName -like "temp-*" |
    Remove-AzResourceGroup -Force

# Export cmdlet output to CSV
Get-AzVM | Select-Object Name, Location,
    @{N="Size";E={$_.HardwareProfile.VmSize}} |
    Export-Csv -Path vm-inventory.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Export to JSON
Get-AzResourceGroup | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3 | Out-File rg-list.json

# Format as a table for display (terminal only — loses object type)
Get-AzVM | Format-Table Name, Location,
    @{N="Size";E={$_.HardwareProfile.VmSize}} -AutoSize
Error Handling with Az Cmdlets
Networking Cmdlets

# List virtual networks
Get-AzVirtualNetwork -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object Name, Location, @{N="AddressSpace";E={$_.AddressSpace.AddressPrefixes}}

# Create a virtual network with a subnet
$subnet = New-AzVirtualNetworkSubnetConfig -Name "default" -AddressPrefix "10.0.0.0/24"
$vnet = New-AzVirtualNetwork `
    -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -Location eastus `
    -Name myVNet `
    -AddressPrefix "10.0.0.0/16" `
    -Subnet $subnet

# List public IP addresses
Get-AzPublicIpAddress -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object Name, IpAddress, PublicIpAllocationMethod

# Create a public IP
$pip = New-AzPublicIpAddress `
    -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -Location eastus `
    -Name myPublicIp `
    -AllocationMethod Static `
    -Sku Standard

# List network security groups and their rules
Get-AzNetworkSecurityGroup -ResourceGroupName myRG | ForEach-Object {
    $nsg = $_
    $nsg.SecurityRules | Select-Object `
        @{N="NSG";E={$nsg.Name}}, Name, Priority, Access, Direction, Protocol,
        SourceAddressPrefix, DestinationPortRange
}
App Service and Web App Cmdlets

# List all web apps in a resource group
Get-AzWebApp -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object Name, DefaultHostName, State, Kind

# Get the app settings of a web app
$app = Get-AzWebApp -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myApp
$app.SiteConfig.AppSettings | Select-Object Name, Value

# Set / add app settings
$settings = @{
    "ENVIRONMENT"     = "Production"
    "MAX_CONNECTIONS" = "200"
    "LOG_LEVEL"       = "Warning"
}
Set-AzWebApp -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myApp -AppSettings $settings

# Start / Stop / Restart a web app
Start-AzWebApp   -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myApp
Stop-AzWebApp    -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myApp
Restart-AzWebApp -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myApp

# Get streaming logs
Get-AzWebAppPublishingProfile -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myApp `
    -Format WebDeploy -OutputFile ".\publish.xml"

# Scale up: change the App Service Plan SKU
Set-AzAppServicePlan -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myPlan `
    -Tier "PremiumV3" -WorkerSize Small
RBAC and Role Assignment Cmdlets

# List built-in role definitions
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object IsCustom -eq $false |
    Select-Object Name, Id | Sort-Object Name

# Check what roles a user has in a subscription
Get-AzRoleAssignment -SignInName user@contoso.com |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope

# Assign a built-in role to a service principal
New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId $sp.Id `
    -RoleDefinitionName "Storage Blob Data Contributor" `
    -Scope "/subscriptions/$subId/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa"

# List all role assignments in a resource group
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, ObjectType

# Remove a role assignment
Remove-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId $sp.Id `
    -RoleDefinitionName "Storage Blob Data Contributor" `
    -Scope "/subscriptions/$subId/resourceGroups/myRG/..."

# List deny assignments (Azure Policy / Blueprint locks)
Get-AzDenyAssignment -ResourceGroupName myRG
Deployment and ARM Template Cmdlets

# Deploy a Bicep or ARM template to a resource group
New-AzResourceGroupDeployment `
    -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -TemplateFile ".\main.bicep" `
    -TemplateParameterObject @{
        storageAccountName = "mysa123"
        location           = "eastus"
        sku                = "Standard_LRS"
    }

# Validate without deploying (What-If for Bicep/ARM)
$whatIf = New-AzResourceGroupDeployment `
    -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -TemplateFile ".\main.bicep" `
    -WhatIf

# Use Test-AzResourceGroupDeployment for dry-run validation
$errors = Test-AzResourceGroupDeployment `
    -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -TemplateFile ".\main.bicep" `
    -TemplateParameterFile ".\main.parameters.json"

if ($errors) {
    Write-Error "Template validation failed:"
    $errors | ForEach-Object { Write-Error $_.Message }
    exit 1
}

# List all deployments in a resource group
Get-AzResourceGroupDeployment -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object DeploymentName, ProvisioningState, Timestamp |
    Sort-Object Timestamp -Descending | Select-Object -First 10
Monitoring and Activity Log Cmdlets

# Get the activity log for a resource group over the last 24 hours
$start = (Get-Date).AddHours(-24)
Get-AzActivityLog -ResourceGroupName myRG -StartTime $start |
    Select-Object EventTimestamp, OperationName, Status, Caller |
    Sort-Object EventTimestamp -Descending

# Get metrics for a resource (e.g. VM CPU)
$resourceId = (Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM).Id
$metrics = Get-AzMetric -ResourceId $resourceId `
    -MetricName "Percentage CPU" `
    -StartTime (Get-Date).AddHours(-1) `
    -EndTime (Get-Date) `
    -TimeGrain "00:05:00"

$metrics.Data | Select-Object TimeStamp,
    @{N="CPU%";E={[math]::Round($_.Average, 2)}}

# List metric definitions for a resource type
Get-AzMetricDefinition -ResourceId $resourceId |
    Select-Object Name, Unit | Sort-Object Name

# Get alerts configured in a resource group
Get-AzMetricAlertRuleV2 -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object Name, Severity, Enabled
Batch and Parallel Operations
PowerShell's ForEach-Object -Parallel (PowerShell 7+) combined with Az cmdlets enables concurrent operations across multiple resources, dramatically reducing run time for bulk tasks.

Use -ThrottleLimit to cap concurrency. Azure API throttling kicks in around 1200 read requests per 5 minutes per subscription — parallel operations at high ThrottleLimit values can hit this limit. Start with ThrottleLimit 5–10.
Summary

Az cmdlets follow the Verb-AzNoun pattern—knowing the resource noun (AzVM, AzStorageAccount, AzKeyVaultSecret) and the standard verbs (Get, New, Set, Update, Remove, Start, Stop, Invoke) lets you predict most cmdlet names. Use Get-Command and Get-Help to discover and read them. Cmdlets return .NET objects that compose naturally with Where-Object, Select-Object, Sort-Object, and other pipeline verbs, enabling concise filtering, projection, and aggregation without text parsing. Use -ErrorAction Stop with try/catch blocks for robust error handling in automation scripts.
